message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  An improved understanding of human T-cell-mediated immunity in COVID-19 is important if we are to optimize therapeutic and vaccine strategies. Experience with influenza shows that infection primes CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cell memory to shared peptides presented by common HLA types like HLA-A2. Following re-infection, cross-reactive CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cells enhance recovery and diminish clinical severity. Stimulating peripheral blood mononuclear cells from COVID-19 convalescent patients with overlapping peptides from SARS-CoV-2 Spike, Nucleocapsid and Membrane proteins led to the clonal expansion of SARS-CoV-2-specific CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cells
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  , with CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  sets being typically robust. For CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cells taken directly
                  <jats:italic>ex vivo</jats:italic>
                  , we identified two HLA-A*02:01-restricted SARS-CoV-2 epitopes, A2/S
                  <jats:sub>269–277</jats:sub>
                  and A2/Orf1ab
                  <jats:sub>3183–3191</jats:sub>
                  . Using peptide-HLA tetramer enrichment, direct
                  <jats:italic>ex vivo</jats:italic>
                  assessment of the A2/S
                  <jats:sub>269</jats:sub>
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and A2/Orf1ab
                  <jats:sub>3183</jats:sub>
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  populations indicated that the more prominent A2/S
                  <jats:sub>269</jats:sub>
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  set was detected at comparable frequency (∼1.3×10
                  <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                  ) in acute and convalescent HLA-A*02:01
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  patients. But, while the numbers were higher than those found in uninfected HLA-A*02:01
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  donors
                  <jats:italic>(∼</jats:italic>
                  2.5×10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  ), they were low when compared with frequencies for influenza-specific (A2/M1
                  <jats:sub>58</jats:sub>
                  ) and EBV-specific (A2/BMLF
                  <jats:sub>1280</jats:sub>
                  )
                  <jats:italic>(∼</jats:italic>
                  1.38×10
                  <jats:sup>−4</jats:sup>
                  ) populations. Phenotypic analysis
                  <jats:italic>ex vivo</jats:italic>
                  of A2/S
                  <jats:sub>269</jats:sub>
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cells from COVID-19 convalescents showed that A2/S
                  <jats:sub>269</jats:sub>
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cells were predominantly negative for the CD38, HLA-DR, PD-1 and CD71 activation markers, although the majority of total CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cells were granzyme and/or perforin-positive. Furthermore, the bias towards naïve, stem cell memory and central memory A2/S
                  <jats:sub>269</jats:sub>
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cells rather than effector memory populations suggests that SARS-CoV2 infection may be compromising CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cell activation. Priming with an appropriate vaccine may thus have great value for optimizing protective CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cell immunity in COVID-19.
                </jats:p>
title:
Suboptimal SARS-CoV-2-specific CD8
                  <sup>+</sup>
                  T-cell response associated with the prominent HLA-A*02:01 phenotype

reference.unstructured: E. K. Stokes et al. (2020) Coronavirus Disease 2019 Case Surveillance — United States, January 22–May 30, 2020. (MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep 2020), pp 759–765.

reference.article-title: Antibody responses to SARS-CoV-2 in patients with COVID-19
Breadth of concomitant immune responses prior to patient recovery: a case report of non-severe COVID-19
Understanding CD8(+) T-cell responses toward the native and alternate HLA-A*02:01-restricted WT1 epitope
The ABC of Major Histocompatibility Complexes and T Cell Receptors in Health and Disease
Towards identification of immune and genetic correlates of severe influenza disease in Indigenous Australians
